text:
INTRODUCTION
STATUTORY AUTHORITY
Soil and water conservation districts across the nation were formed as a response to the
environmental disaster cause by the Dust Bowl of the 1930s. Established in 1947, the Valencia Soil
and Water Conservation District received its operational license under a Certificate of Due
Organization, issued by the Secretary of State of New Mexico. On May 1, 1947, the Certificate
declared, “the Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District is hereby made a body corporate
and politic with full power to exercise all rights and privileges provided by law.” Valencia Soil and
Water Conservation District is one of 47 conservation districts in New Mexico.
In New Mexico, soil and water conservation districts (SWCDs) are independent subdivisions of
state government. Their statutory authority derives from the Soil and Water Conservation District
Act (Section 73-20-25 through Section 73-20-48 NMSA 1978), a law of the State of New Mexico
[hereafter, the District Act].
Under state law, SWCDs promote the responsible use and conservation of natural resources for
the benefit of the people, animals, and economy of New Mexico. The District Act outlines specific
measures for natural resource conservation, wildlife preservation, flood mitigation, and the wellbeing of New Mexicans. Such measures include controlling and preventing soil erosion; preventing
floodwater and sediment damage; furthering the conservation, development, beneficial
application, and proper disposal of water; and promoting the use of impounded water for
recreation, propagation of fish and wildlife, irrigation, and urban and industrial needs. SWCDs
partner with local, state, federal, and private partners to conduct natural resource conservation
education, promotion, and implementation.
GOVERNANCE
The Valencia SWCD is governed by a 7-member Board of Supervisors. Five Board members are
local landowners and residents, elected by the registered voters of the District during local
elections (held every 4 years on odd-numbered years). The 2 appointed Board members are
traditionally reserved for representatives of the Pueblo of Isleta and the Pueblo of Laguna. The
District nominates and votes for the 2 appointed members, whose appointments are approved
by the New Mexico Soil and Water Conservation Commission.
REACH
The VSWCD serves 91,000 residents in 2 Pueblos and 5 counties of central New Mexico along
the Middle Rio Grande (MRG) and the influent Rio Puerco, in a catchment area the size of
Delaware. Among 47 conservation districts in New Mexico, Valencia SWCD uniquely holds
substantial land in conservation land—279 acres along the MRG river. One preserve is open to
the public (Whitfield Wildlife Conservation Area) and 2 others should open in Fiscal Year 2027.
MISSION
The Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District provides education,
technical assistance, and recognition to current and future land and
water stewards in the conservation of natural resources, including
water, soil, plants, and wildlife.
VISION
The Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District provides natural
resource conservation for a quality environment demonstrated through
active leadership, cooperation, and partnership.
GUIDING PRINCIPLES
Valencia SWCD values:
• natural resources
• soil and water conservation
• agriculture
• wildlife
• cooperation and partnership
• District staff, Board members, and residents
• the Friends of Whitfield, a non-profit, tax-exempt organization
 that supports VSWCD programming at Whitfield
 Conservation Lands
• the first stewards of the lands in this District: the members of the Pueblo of Isleta, the Pueblo of Laguna, and their
 ancestors
RESOLVE
Valencia SWCD will:
• support practical, voluntary solutions to natural resource problems.
• provide quality services.
• promote strong partnerships and teamwork.
• transfer the best available technology about natural resource conservation.
• advocate natural resource stewardship.
• offer hands-on, outdoor science learning in natural environments, including at Whitfield Wildlife
 Conservation lands and along the Middle Rio Grande bosque.
• continue restoration of Whitfield Wildlife Conservation land to mimic the historic ecosystem mosaic along
 the riparian corridor of the Middle Rio Grande, with salt grass meadows, increased biodiversity in the
 bosque, and enhanced wetlands, for wildlife habitat, environmental education, and outdoor recreation.
• maintain the native grassland at the Stacy Unit for wildlife habitat and outdoor recreation.
• manage the Rio Abajo Conservation Area to provide habitat for wildlife, new outdoor recreation
 opportunities for the public (e.g., canoeing, fishing, kayaking), and environmental education about adaptive
 management, forest thinning (of invasive species), and fire mitigation.

section: GENERAL DESCRIPTION OF LANDS WITHIN VALENCIA SWCD
text:
GENERAL DESCRIPTION OF LANDS WITHIN VALENCIA SWCD

The elevation within the District ranges from 4,700
feet at the Rio Grande valley to about 10,000 feet
at the crest of the Manzano Mountains.
Soils of the flood plains vary from shallow to deep,
depending on the alluvial materials from which
they have been formed. They range in texture
from sand to clay but are generally calcareous. As
the depth of the water table from the surface
varies from a few inches to a few feet, depth
should be considered for any agricultural
undertaking as well as current urbanization in the
Rio Grande flood plain area.
Moderately deep soils generally underlain by a caliche horizon occupy the plains extending from the valleys to the base of the
mountains. At present, these soils are utilized as grazing lands. Both Aeolian (wind) and water erosion is evident in the mesas
above the Rio Grande flood plain. Desertification is occurring in some areas where vegetative cover is inadequate or has been
removed. On land west of I-25, off Route 6 in Los Lunas, erosion is accelerating on barren soil, causing dust storms that obscure
visibility on the roadways, reduce property values, and cause or exacerbate lung disease of residents in the vicinity. The depth
of the water table on the mesas above the rivers within the District and further upland can be up to 400 feet.
Mountainous areas are occupied by rough stony lands and moderately deep soils on steep to gentle slopes and along the
narrow valleys in the mountains. They are used mainly for grazing, woodcutting, and outdoor recreation. Slight to moderate
water and Aeolian erosion occur in these areas.
The cover on the rangeland is dominantly galleta, grama, and dropseed grasses on the lower levels. In the foothills and
mountains areas, the cover is grama grasses and piñon-juniper woodland, with scattered ponderosa pines in the higher
elevations. On overgrazed areas, the grasses have been replaced by snakeweed, needle grasses, weeds, and bare soil.
In the Rio Grande Valley, the vega grasses are mostly alkali sacaton and salt grass. The riparian forest—the bosque—includes
native cottonwoods and desert willow trees, as well as an invasive understory of salt cedar, Russian olive, and Chinese elm.
The annual rainfall is very limited in the lower elevations, about eight inches; in the higher elevations the rainfall is somewhat
higher, at 12 inches plus. Approximately forty percent of the rainfall comes during the monsoons in July, August, and September.
Changing climate, however, may bring less rainfall, reduced snow mass in northern New Mexico, with less spring runoff into
the Rio Grande, and increased temperature. Catastrophic flooding is expected, as well—possibly worse than the flood events
of the 100-year and 200-year magnitude that hit Belen and communities to the south in 2018 and 2021.
The growing season averages 183 days. The average date of the last killing frost is April 20; the average date of the first killing
frost is October 20. The average lake evaporation is 68 inches per year.
The principal urban centers are Los Lunas, Belen, and Bosque Farms. Urbanizing municipalities include Peralta and the City of
Rio Communities. Many villages are within the District boundaries, including Tomé, Bosque, Veguita, and La Joya; Isleta Village
Proper and Chicale in the Pueblo of Isleta; and Encinal, Mesita, Laguna, Paguate, Paraje, and Seama in the Pueblo of Laguna.
section_path: GENERAL DESCRIPTION OF LANDS WITHIN VALENCIA SWCD

text:
NATURAL RESOURCE SURVEY METHODOLOGY

To enable the District to meet objectives outlined in the District Act, Valencia Soil and Water
Conservation District must understand the current natural resource concerns of its
stakeholders. Accordingly, Valencia SWCD contracted an expert to write a survey, calculate
results, and write a long-range conservation plan informed by survey results. Valencia SWCD
requested the author of its prior long-range plan, Cliff Sánchez, to write survey questions
about the natural resource issues and concerns of the people and agencies within the
District boundaries, to analyze the results, and write a new conservation plan.
Twenty-five years ago, most soil and water conservation districts maintained
a list of “cooperators,” residents who actively engaged with their districts.
As that practice faded, the District had to approximate engagement. It
would have been cost-prohibitive to attempt to survey the estimated 91,000
District residents, primarily because postage alone would have amounted
to at least $61,880. Instead, Valencia SWCD landed on the idea of estimating
District interest by those who voted in the 2023 District election on
November 7, 2023 (the last local election). Although there were 11,277
votes casted in that election for both at-large and landowner Board
members, only 8,474 individuals voted. Valencia SWSCD mailed a
postcard to the 8,474 voters, inviting them to participate in a survey
of natural resource issues and concerns. Only 3 votes were cast
among the 103 registered voters within Socorro County who resided
within Valencia SWCD’s boundaries at the time of the 2023 election.
Because the District wanted to include their voices in this survey,
all currently registered voters in Socorro County who are residents
(n = 69) also received a postcard. In total, Valencia SWCD mailed 8,543 postcards.
The survey was accessible by scanning a QR code on the postcard. For those who could not
use the QR code, they were either mailed, emailed, or delivered the survey. The postcard
was mailed by a union printer in Albuquerque. The District sought and received a grant from
the Land of Enchantment Legacy Fund, administered by the New Mexico Department of
Agriculture, for the survey and report.
The District envisioned that the new conservation plan would include relevant information
from the prior plan (written in 1997); analysis of the current natural resource base in the
District, including existing conditions and trends for each natural resource; pertinent
information gathered from the Valencia SWCD and others; analysis of conservation needs
and priorities; and data-driven identification of the natural resource concerns of the
residents of the District (from the survey results).

NATURAL RESOURCE SURVEY RESULTS
On April 18, 2025, the Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District mailed a postcard to 8,543 District residents, inviting them
to participate in a survey regarding natural resource concerns and issues. The survey closed on May 2, 2025. There were 268
respondents.
Most respondents (n = 260) completed the survey by accessing the QR code on the postcard. Seven people emailed their
survey to the District at public-input@valenciaswcd-nm.gov. One person turned in the survey in person to District staff at
Whitfield Wildlife Conservation Area.
The first four questions asked respondents to indicate their top five answers to each question. Because the first question
included a near tie in the fifth ranking (71 and 70), six answers were listed. Of the remaining twelve questions, 10 were binary—
requiring either yes or no responses.
QUESTION 1. The top six natural resource concerns and issues within the communities of the Valencia Soil and Water
Conservation District are:
1. aquifers; depletion of groundwater from aquifers
2. water quantity: scarcity of water (as affected by increasing aridity/drought, decreasing snow mass and rain, and
 increasing temperature) and water access (including water rights and water theft)
3. agricultural land preservation
4. pollution of air, land, or water
5. land use changes (urbanization and reduced rural land)
6. bosque (riparian forest) and forest management

The next ten areas of importance are:
7. invasive plants, pests, and noxious weeds
 8. chemicals: use and management of fertilizers, herbicides, and pesticides
9. agriculture: sustainable food and fiber production
10. air quality and atmospheric change
11. erosion of topsoil from natural forces (wind, water, ice) or from human activities (construction, deforestation,
 irrigation, mining, overgrazing, tillage, and urbanization)
12. natural disasters: catastrophic wildfires, extreme flooding, persistent drought
13. wildlife population, including pollinators
14. water quality (including impacts from animal nutrients, chemicals, and stormwater)
15. habitat: fragmentation and loss of habitat for wildlife
16. stormwater management and flood control

QUESTION 2. The top five groups that would benefit from information, conservation products/goods, or technical
assistance from the Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District:
1. Valencia County residents
2. backyard Gardeners
3. homeowners
4. beginning farmers
5. full-time farmers

The next ten groups that would benefit are:
 7. land-use planners
 8. schools
 9. 4H programs and Future Farmers of America (FFA)
 10. small-acreage farmers
 11. acequia associations
 12. land developers
 13. part-time farmers
 14. government agencies or elected officials
 15. recreational user groups (bicycle riders, birders, canoers, equestrians, hikers, kayakers, photographers, runners,
 and walkers, etc.)
In 1997, the top five groups were developers, state and local politicians, full-time farmers/ranchers, part-time farmers/ranchers,
and environmental groups.

QUESTION 3. The five most important programs and services offered by the Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District are:
1. education programs for children held outdoors at the Whitfield Wildlife Conservation Area (hands-on outdoor
 science learning)
2. forestry thinning by the removal of invasive species in the bosque
3. bosque restoration and protection at Whitfield Wildlife Conservation Area
4. financial assistance to implement conservation practices (aimed at small-acreage farmers and backyard gardeners)
5. financial assistance for conservation at home (gutter systems, rain barrels, water-conserving toilets, etc.)

The next ten important programs and services offered by Valencia SWCD are:
 6. field days on soil health practices (held in communities throughout the Valencia SWCD)
7. conservation planning for landowners or those leasing land
8. rainwater harvesting
9. endangered and threatened species habitat at Whitfield Wildlife Conservation Area
10. land use planning
11. Wildlife habitat enhancement (for deer, elk, wild turkeys, other mammals, etc.)
12. soil testing and physical assessment of soil
13. subdivision reviews (to address soil erosion, stormwater flooding, and sediment deposition that result from land-
 disturbance activities)
14. good governance
15. job training [in conservation (Youth Conservation Corps), firefighting (on-site training from Valencia County Fire
 Department), first aid, soil health, etc.]
In 1997, the top six programs and services were conservation planning; soil survey and soil information; engineering design; costshare programs (financial assistance); rural development assistance; and erosion and sediment control.

section: NATURAL RESOURCES MANAGEMENT AND PROTECTION Overview & GOAL A
text:
NATURAL RESOURCES MANAGEMENT AND PROTECTION
The following natural resource goals and priority actions for the District were informed by District residents who participated
in the survey. To support and advocate for the proper management and conservation of natural resources requires community
involvement. The District is grateful to those residents who participated in the survey.
GOAL A – Water conservation and management.
Water is a critical life-sustaining resource. The 50-year forecast for New Mexico is a hotter and dryer climate with reduced
snow mass, meaning water must be conserved and preserved for the use of current and future generations. Water quality is
the principal measure of how people have treated the land.
Action 1. Aquifers:
Install dry inflow wells in the bottom of all flood control and runoff ponds throughout the District. These generally would be
large-diameter, rock-filled, and filtered inflow wells to recharge aquifers.
Action 2. Aquifers:
Advocate for a detailed hydrological study of aquifers in the District to assess the condition and health of the groundwater
resource. This scientific data will reveal the current depletion rate and annual recharge per year.
Action 3. Aquifers:
Work with the District’s long-term partner, the Bosque Ecosystem Monitoring Program, to study the connectivity of Rio Grande
flows, farm irrigation drains, and open canals with the groundwater and vice versa. This work can indicate where aquifer
recharge and depletion are occurring. 
Action 4. Water Needs:
Provide public information on agriculture, livestock, and residential water needs, the
impacts of scarcity, and the methods and benefits of conservation.
Action 5. Water Efficiencies:
Continue assisting irrigators on improving irrigation system efficiencies through
technological advances and improved management.
Action 6. Water Quantity:
Using resources from the U.S. Department of Agriculture’s Natural Resource
Conservation Service (NRCS), the New Mexico Department of Agriculture (NMDA), and
Valencia SWCD, assist a significant number of agricultural producers and landowners
in implementing water conservation practices. These could include laser land
leveling, irrigation pipelines, drip irrigation systems, concrete lined ditches, improved
soil health, gutter systems with rain barrels, and rain gardens—all methods to
conserve our most limited resource—water.
Action 7. Greywater:
Actively encourage the use of greywater for backyard gardening.
Action 8. Alkali Waters:
Implement methods to improve alkali waters from springs and wells.
Action 9. Rainwater Harvesting:
Assist backyard gardeners, homeowners, and others in methods of rainwater
harvesting, through gutter systems and rain barrels, for application to backyard
gardens, pollinator plants, etc.
section_path: NATURAL RESOURCES MANAGEMENT AND PROTECTION Overview & GOAL A

section: GOAL B – Agricultural land preservation and land use changes
text:
GOAL B – Agricultural land preservation and land use changes.
Maintaining agricultural lands, green space, and land in conservation can help limit
urban sprawl and maintain a rural character within Valencia SWCD.

Action 1. Local Work Group Meeting:
These public meeting(s) are convened on at least a yearly basis by Valencia SWCD
and its principal partner, the Natural Resource Conservation Service (NRCS) of the
U.S. Department of Agriculture. The District should publicize the meeting and seek
to expand participation to include more District landowners and land users, as well
as lawmakers. In this manner, the public can encourage their legislators about the
need to protect farmlands and conserve land and water resources. 
Action 2. Legislative Forum:
Annually sponsor a legislative forum for local, state, and
federal legislators.
Action 3. Agricultural Land Preservation:
Work with the Rio Grande Agricultural Land Trust organization and other conservation easement organizations
and programs to enroll farmlands, which can preserve
agricultural land and convey tax incentives to enrolled
landowners. Encourage NRCS to provide more funding
for conservation easements and explore tax incentives
and special assessments to facilitate this.
Action 4. Agricultural Land Preservation:
Provide facts and figures to municipalities and villages within Valencia and Socorro Counties and to Valencia and Socorro
County Governments in support of the preservation of land in agriculture.
Action 5. Agricultural Land Preservation:
Assist Valencia and Socorro Counties and municipalities with master plans, land use plans, and regulations and ordinances
that will protect farmland.
Action 6. Agricultural Land Preservation:
Designate a District representative to attend meetings of the Valencia County Commission and its Planning and Zoning
Commission to discourage unconsidered losses of Agricultural lands.
Action 7. Agricultural Land Preservation:
Assist the Pueblos of Isleta and Laguna in maintaining and managing farmlands to keep lands in production, on their request. 
Action 8. Subdivision Reviews:
Re-engage with Valencia and Socorro Counties and with local
municipalities—as required by state law—in subdivision reviews
for proper terrain and water management. Valencia SWCD will
evaluate plans to prevent or address soil erosion, stormwater
flooding, and sediment deposition that can result from landdisturbance activities. Valencia SWCD can work to ensure that
ordinances and rules are in place to require a “District-reviewed”
stamp before subdivisions are approved and construction
begins, in keeping with many jurisdictions across the nation.
section_path: GOAL B – Agricultural land preservation and land use changes

section: GOAL C — Mitigation and prevention of air, land, and water pollution
text:
GOAL C — Mitigation and prevention of air, land, and water pollution

Action 1. Pesticides, herbicides, and fertilizers:
Discourage the use of chemicals in agriculture and encourage the transition to
regenerative, sustainable agriculture. Provide information about the proper use, storage,
and disposal of such chemicals.
Action 2. Water:
Educate the public on the importance of installing wellhead protection.
Action 3. Wetlands:
Encourage the protection of existing wetlands. Inform the public of their importance,
especially in this hot and dry desert environment.
Action 4. Wetland Restoration:
Assist in reconstructing wetlands as a means of reducing stormwater pollution. Grass
in restored wetlands can perform biofiltration, whereby the blades of the grass filter
water, absorb pollutants and nutrients, and prevent contaminants from reaching the
groundwater. In addition, polluted water that passes through the healthy soil of restored
wetlands is filtered by plant roots and soil microbes, which begin the breakdown of
toxins and continue clearing pollutants, in a process called phytoremediation.
Action 5. Watersheds:
Actively participate in projects along the Middle Rio Grande Watershed by providing assistance for watershed management
through whole-farm and whole-ranch planning and installation.
Action 6. Noxious Weeds:
Noxious weeds are those tenacious and insidious plants that invade all lands (even well-managed lands) and cause untold damage
and loss of income. Valencia SWCD will re-engage with the County Noxious Weed Coordinator to quantify and map locations of
noxious weeds and encourage their elimination, by seeking funding from NMDA’s Noxious Weed Program.
section_path: GOAL C — Mitigation and prevention of air, land, and water pollution

section: GOAL D – Bosque (riparian forest) and forest management
text:
GOAL D – Bosque (riparian forest) and
forest management.
Action 1. Bosque Restoration:
Assist with thinning and adaptive management projects in
private and public land along the Middle Rio Grande, especially
at the Whitfield Wildlife Conservation area and its Stacy and
Rio Abajo units. In riparian lands affected by the 2022 Big Hole
Fire, replant with a biodiverse palette of plants and shrubs
that are both fire- and climate-adapted, including via assisted
migration. That is, replant cottonwoods, willows, and other
shrubs obtained from southern New Mexico and return
species that once thrived in the bosque. This work also entails
removing invasive species.
Action 2. Bosque Management:
Assist the Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District to develop and implement a bosque management plan for riparian land
under its jurisdiction. The plan should include restoration, as outlined in Action 1, immediately above.
Action 3. Fire Prevention:
Educate landowners about thinning thick, overgrown, or invasive vegetation on
their properties and about fire-hardening their homes and out-buildings. Educate
all land users about the prevention of forest fire and encourage buffers at
wildland—urban interface areas. These methods can prevent or slow down wildfires
and reduce the number of human-caused fires along the Middle Rio Grande and
other areas throughout the Valencia SWCD.
Action 4. Woodlands:
Assist with improved
piñon-juniper (P-J) woodland management. P-J
resource planning; P-J
stand reduction on
areas where encroachment is occurring;
training landowners to
determine proper cutting and harvesting of the right trees for fuelwood, posts, and stays; and providing
P-J mast for wildlife are a means to this end.
section_path: GOAL D – Bosque (riparian forest) and forest management

section: GOAL E – Mitigation and prevention of natural and human-caused erosion.
text:
GOAL E – Mitigation and prevention of natural and human-caused erosion.
Action 1. Erosion Prevention:
Directly assist District residents in preventing erosion by providing natural resource conservation plans that can help increase
their likelihood of success—in farming, gardening, ranching, and in maintaining their residential property, while improving soil
health and its water filtration capacity.
Action 2. Financial Assistance Program:
Provide financial assistance to landowners (or land users, with the consent of the landowner) in installing a wide array of
practical conservation practices that prevent erosion and improve soil health and water conservation. The District’s standard
program, aimed at small-acreage farmers and backyard gardeners, provides financial assistance to implement conservation
practices on their land and must have a natural resource conservation plan. The stand-alone program facilitates conservation
at home (gutter systems, rain barrels, water-conserving toilets, etc.), without a formal conservation plan.
Action 3. Plants:
Promote the seeding of grasses, planting of shrubs and trees, and the establishment of windbreaks and hedgerows for erosion
control and wildlife habitat on public, private, and Pueblo lands.
Action 4. Off-Road Vehicles:
Educate the public on the potential for severe erosion caused by the careless use of off-road vehicles (ATVs). Encourage ATVs
to travel only on established roads and trails to protect vital natural resources.
Action 5. Severe Flooding Events:
Collaborate with the new Valencia County Arroyo Flood Control District, Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District, Natural Resource
Conservation Service, and with the counties, municipalities, and Pueblos served by the District in planning thorough floodcontrol projects that protect people, their lives, and livelihoods from catastrophic flooding.
Action 6. Subdivision Reviews:
Re-engage with Valencia and Socorro Counties and with
local municipalities—as required by state law—in subdivision
reviews for proper terrain and water management. Valencia
SWCD will evaluate plans to prevent or address soil erosion,
stormwater flooding, and sediment deposition that can
result from land-disturbance activities. Valencia SWCD can
work to ensure that ordinances and rules are in place to
require a “District-reviewed” stamp before subdivisions are
approved and construction begins, in keeping with many
jurisdictions across the nation.
section_path: GOAL E – Mitigation and prevention of natural and human-caused erosion.

section: GOAL F – Soil health, education and outreach, and other issues that affect quality of life.
text:
GOAL F – Soil health, education and outreach, and other issues that affect quality of life.
Quality of Life is directly related to the amount of open lands and green
belts in communities and to the quantity and quality of natural resources.
Action 1. Mitigate and prevent dust storms to improve air quality:
Recommence subdivision reviews and ensure that ordinances and rules in
place to require a “District-reviewed” stamp before subdivisions are
approved and construction begins, in keeping with many jurisdictions across
the nation. Engage with land developers and industrial builders to provide
referrals to qualified terrain and water management planners for temporary
and permanent erosion control practices. Implementing such practices prior
to construction prevents serious dust storms and poor air quality.
Action 2. Soil Management:
Educate agricultural producers on soil health improvement practices of crop rotations, cropping systems, residue use, no-till
or low-till farming, green manure crops, and integration of animals and microorganisms in land management.
Action 3. Soil Management:
Increase awareness of managing saline and alkali soils, and soils with poor drainage.
Action 4. Soil Health:
Actively assist small-acreage producers, backyard gardeners, and all those interested in the science of soil health principles;
and how to implement soil health practices to greatly improve productivity and conserve natural resources.
Action 5. Soil Stewardship:
Participate in soil stewardship activities, including sponsoring District residents who seek to become national Soil Health
Champions.
Action 6. Range management on Pueblo lands:
Assist Grazing Associations and ranchers with range management practice implementation based on the plans provided by
NRCS and others, on request.
Action 7. Agriculture producers enrolled in the Pueblos:
Assist the agricultural producers of the Isleta and Laguna Pueblos with conservation planning and healthy soil practice
implementation, on request.
Action 8. Range Management:
Assist ranchers with implementation of important conservation practices of cross-fencing, stock water development, methods
to reduce water evaporation from stock tanks, planned grazing systems, and more.
Action 9. Range Management Education:
Provide field trips to intense grazing management programs, for ranchers and other interested District residents.
Action 10. Dan Goodman Scholarships:
Promote applications for the Dan Goodman Soil and Water Conservation and Environmental Improvement Award among high
school students (grades 9-12) residing within the Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District boundaries. The award aims to
reward one or two students per year who have studied about or worked in conservation or the environment.
Action 11. Outreach:
Continue to publish columns in local newspapers [e.g., Valencia County News-Bulletin
and el Defensór-Chieftain (in Socorro)], posts on social media, and news releases for
other media outlets (radio, television, and text-blast services) about soil and water
conservation, District programs, and special events.
Action 12. Outreach:
Prepare and distribute an annual report.
Action 13. Outreach:
Promote conservation awareness contests, such as the yearly Envirothon, poster
contests, and essay contests.
Action 14. Whitfield Wildlife Conservation Area:
Continue to manage the preserve for green space and wildlife habitat, outdoor
recreation, and hands-on, outdoor environmental education.
Action 15. Whitfield Outreach:
Provide educational and training workshops, tours, outdoor science learning
for school children and adult learners, and wildlife viewing.
Action 16. VSWCD Outreach:
Encourage attendance by the public and government agencies at District Board meetings.
section_path: GOAL F – Soil health, education and outreach, and other issues that affect quality of life.

section: GOAL G – Wildlife and fisheries habitat
text:
GOAL G – Wildlife and fisheries habitat.
The presence of fish and wildlife is a barometer of healthy ecosystems. Wildlife includes big game, small game, reptiles and
amphibians, small mammals, game birds, waterfowl, songbirds, fur bearing animals, fish, and endangered species.
Most soil and water conservation practices contribute to improved wildlife habitat. Additional planning and some modifications
can make many areas within the District safe for native and migratory wildlife.
Action 1. Pollinator Plants:
Promote the establishment of pollinator gardens with flowers, vines, shrubs, and trees
to enhance populations of pollinator bees, birds, and other insects.

Action 2. Riparian Zones:
Work with individuals, groups, and the Pueblos to improve riparian
corridors along the Rio Grande, Rio Puerco, and the Rio San José.
Assist with riparian management planning and restoration.
Action 3. Browse Species:
Assist landowners in managing and enhancing important browse
species for increasing cover, feed, and wildlife populations.
Action 4. Habitat Needs:
Encourage landowners to consider wildlife needs for feed, water, cover, and
space in all of their operations.
Action 5. Fisheries:
Assist landowners in the design and construction of ponds, restoration of
wetlands, and in the identification of vernal pools and marshes.
Action 6. Fisheries:
Educate the public on how soil and water conservation practices can
maintain or improve fisheries habitats.
Action 7. Water Quality:
Assist with the protection of water quality that is potable for humans, safe
for animals, and fit for fish habitat.
Action 8. Cooperation:
Stimulate cooperative planning and application efforts on projects so that
the needs of fish and wildlife can be met harmoniously with agricultural and
domestic uses. Establish safe crossings for animals to access water, food,
and habitat.
Action 9. Instream Flows:
Take an active role in the discussion and decisions of permanent instream flows in the Rio Grande corridor.
Action 10. Threatened and Endangered Species:
Work with the Middle Rio Grande Endangered Species Collaborative and the Middle Rio Grande Conservancy District to improve
habitat for threatened and endangered species along the Rio Grande corridor.
section_path: GOAL G – Wildlife and fisheries habitat

text:
EPILOGUE
The conservation of natural resources can harmonize use with sustainability. What one individual does can directly affect
other humans, wildlife, and entire ecosystems. This Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District’s Long Range Conservation
Plan outlines programs that provide opportunities for all District residents to participate in the conservation of natural
resources for a quality environment.
Promoting and practicing natural resource conservation can produce measurable results. To some, it is good clean water; to
others, productive agriculture, abundant wildlife, or clean-living conditions with plenty of surrounding green space. To most,
conservation provides a path of sustaining resources for not just today, but for all the tomorrows.
To learn more, please visit the Valencia Soil and Water Conservation District staff and Board members at the Whitfield Wildlife
Conservation Area at 2424 State Highway 47 in Belen, New Mexico; write to Valencia SWCD at P.O. Box 170, Belen, NM, 87002;
email public-input@valenciaswcd-nm.gov; visit the District website at www.valenciaswcd-nm.gov; or call (505) 864-8914.
